coredata.dc:title: Luminescent carbon quantum dots/nanofibrillated cellulose composite aerogel for monitoring adsorption of heavy metal ions in water
coredata.dc:creator: Song, Zihui
Chen, Xueqi
Gong, Xinchao
Gao, Xing
Dai, Qian
Nguyen, Tat Thang
Guo, Minghui
coredata.dc:description: Water pollution, especially that of heavy metal ions, has always been one of the most important kinds of pollution and has long plagued human production and life. Therefore, how to effectively control pollution of heavy metals in water is an urgent problem for scientists. We have prepared a new carbon quantum dots (CQDs)/nanofibrillated cellulose (NFC) composite aerogel using green chemical synthesis and used it for the adsorption and detection of heavy metal ions (Cr3+) in water. At different pH values, the prepared materials adsorb heavy metal ions (Cr3+) in water, and the fluorescence effect of the CQDs in the material decreases with an increase in the amount of heavy metal ions (Cr3+) that are adsorbed. This indicates that the material can be used for real-time monitoring of adsorption behavior. Our research results provide underlying ideas for guiding control of water pollution and extending the applications of aerogels and CQDs.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon quantum dots (CQDs)
Nanofibrillated cellulose (NFC)
Visual adsorption
Fluorescence
Heavy metal ion (Cr3+)

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: One-step synthesis of CQDs/NFC composite aerogel
Adsorption of heavy metal ions (Cr3+) in water by CQDs/NFC composite aerogel

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Formation and fluorescence effect of carbon quantum dots/composite aerogel: (A) detailed preparation process of composite aerogel and (B) fluorescence microscopy images of composite aerogels in a bright field (left) and with 405 nm excitation (blue), 488 nm excitation (green), and 555 nm excitation (red). (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)
Surface analysis of CQDs: (A) TEM images of CQDs (left) and HR-TEM images (right) and (B) carbon quantum dot size distribution.
Fluorescence properties of CQDs and their composites: (A) PL spectra of CQDs and (B) fluorescence intensity of composite aerogels at different initial concentrations.

Adsorption properties of aerogels: (A) scanning electron micrograph of the fiber structure (left) and fiber inner wall (right) of the carbon quantum dots/composite aerogel. (B) N2 adsorption-desorption isotherm and pore size distribution curve of the carbon quantum dots/composite aerogel.

Chemical analysis of composite aerogel: (A) XPS of composite aerogels (i) and blank aerogels (ii); (B) high-resolution C1s XPS spectra; (C) high-resolution N1s XPS spectra; (D) XRD patterns of composite aerogels (i) and CQDs (ii).

Adsorption application of composite aerogel: (A) Adsorption and detection of toxic heavy metal ions by CQDs/NFC composite aerogel. (B) Optical pictures of aerogel under UV light (365 nm) before (left) and after adsorption (right)(The adsorbed ion is Cr3 +, and the adsorption concentration is 100 ppm).

Analysis of adsorption performance of the composite aerogel: (A) Effects of pH on adsorption performance and fluorescence intensity (solution concentration of 100 ppm); (B) adsorption properties of the composite aerogels at pH of 6; (C) pseudo-second-order adsorption kinetics.

Correlation between fluorescence intensity and adsorption capacity: (A) fluorescence intensity and adsorption properties of composite aerogels with Cr3+ concentrations in the range from 20 to 100 ppm at a pH of 6 and (B) curve of fitting of fluorescence loss ((I0–I)/I0) to adsorption capacity (qe).
Summary of representative detection methods for Cr3+.

row.entry: Specificity, non-toxic, simple operation, low cost, suitable for in-field applications
High sensitivity, wide detection range, high selectivity
Expensive, require complex pre-treatment processes, difficult to operate

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Pseudo-first and quasi-second-order kinetics model parameters of composite aerogel adsorption of Cr3+.

head.ce:title: Luminescent carbon quantum dots/nanofibrillated cellulose composite aerogel for monitoring adsorption of heavy metal ions in water

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Bio-based Material Science and Technology of Ministry of Education, Northeast Forestry University, Harbin, 150040, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Bio-based Material Science and Technology of Ministry of Education

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Key Laboratory of Bio-based Material Science and Technology of Ministry of Education, Northeast Forestry University, Harbin, 150040, P.R. China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Vietnam National University of Forestry, Hanoi, 156220, Viet Nam
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Vietnam National University of Forestry, Hanoi, 156220, Viet Nam

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: These authors contribute equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Water pollution, especially that of heavy metal ions, has always been one of the most important kinds of pollution and has long plagued human production and life. Therefore, how to effectively control pollution of heavy metals in water is an urgent problem for scientists. We have prepared a new carbon quantum dots (CQDs)/nanofibrillated cellulose (NFC) composite aerogel using green chemical synthesis and used it for the adsorption and detection of heavy metal ions (Cr3+) in water. At different pH values, the prepared materials adsorb heavy metal ions (Cr3+) in water, and the fluorescence effect of the CQDs in the material decreases with an increase in the amount of heavy metal ions (Cr3+) that are adsorbed. This indicates that the material can be used for real-time monitoring of adsorption behavior. Our research results provide underlying ideas for guiding control of water pollution and extending the applications of aerogels and CQDs.

ce:section.ce:para: With the rapid development of industry and agriculture, the use of chemical products has increased, and this has led to an increasing amount of chemical waste [1]. Increasingly more toxic metal ionic substances have entered the water environment through various channels, and this causes serious pollution to water bodies. During the discharge of industrial wastewater, a considerable amount of heavy metal elements are deposited in natural waters [2]. These heavy metal ions harm the aquatic ecosystem and have various harmful effects on aquatic organisms; they also affect human health both directly and indirectly [3] through drinking water, skin contact, and food chain pathways. Cr3+ is one of the most toxic heavy metal ions in water, and it is particularly harmful to the human body and the environment. Therefore, how to effectively detect and adsorb Cr3+ in water is important for solving the problem of water pollution. The detection methods of Cr3+ can be broadly divided into three categories [4]: optical detection, spectral detection and electrochemical detection (Table 1 ). Among them, CQDs fluorescent probe detection [5,6], which is a type of optical detection, has the advantages of lower biological toxicity, more environmentally friendliness and higher specificity. Therefore, in recent years, CQDs have received widespread attention as a new type of heavy metal ion detection material.

In addition to detecting Cr3+, removing it from water is also a problem that needs to be addressed. At present, there are many techniques, such as redox, ion exchange [19], chemical deposition, adsorption [20,21], and membrane filtration [22,23], that are used for treating toxic metal wastewater. Adsorption is a common method for treating wastewater that contains Cr3+ in a manner that has high treatment efficiency, simple operation, low energy loss, and relatively good environmental protection. Activated carbon [24] and various adsorption resins [25] are commonly used adsorbents; however, their preparation is somewhat difficult and costly, and their adsorption is not easily observed. Thus, it is necessary to prepare an inexpensive, green, and visually observable adsorbent material.

Aerogel is a special porous material that has extremely low density, large surface area, and low thermal conductivity. To date, the types of aerogels that have been reported include carbon [26,27], silicon [28], metal oxides [29], organic polymers [30,31]. Among the many aerogels, nanofibrillated cellulose (NFC) aerogels [32,33] have been widely studied because of their excellent properties such as their wide range of raw materials, low price, environmentally friendliness, and excellent performance. At the same time, because of the rich amount of functional groups on the surface [34], NFC aerogels are also a good carrier for composite materials. In recent years, many applications, such as superinsulation [35], electrochemical properties [36], and adsorption properties [37,38], have been reported. However, thus far, the visual adsorption function of NFC aerogel has not attracted much attention.

CQDs [39,40] are a novel nanocarbon material that is composed of dispersed spherical particles. CQDs have a size of 10 nm or smaller and have fluorescent properties. There are many methods for preparing CQDs, including electrochemical oxidation [41], arc discharge [42], template methods [43], microwave-assisted methods [44,45], and hydrothermal methods [46–48]. The properties of CQDs are similar to those of traditional semiconductor quantum dots [49,50], but CQDs are simpler to prepare, greener, safer, and less biotoxic [51,52]. Unlike other semiconductor quantum dots, the low toxicity of CQDs means that they have wider applications. For example, they have been applied in cell imaging or in vivo optical imaging [53–56]. More importantly, because of their fluorescence quenching effect [57], they have broad application prospects in sensors, especially metal sensing [58]. In the preparation process, new functional groups can be introduced to prepared CQDs [59,60] via the addition of different raw materials, and this leads to CQDs that have different properties. It also makes it possible to make composite CQDs with other materials [61–63].

In this article, we chose to construct a visually observable adsorption material via a simple one-step method that uses NFC aerogel as the framework structure to support CQDs. The main raw materials for CQDs and NFC aerogels are cellulose, which is extracted from wood. Thus, this process is environmentally friendly and nontoxic. Simultaneously, whether CQDs or NFC aerogels are prepared, they are essentially carbon nanomaterials, and the preparation materials are the same. Also, the product contains a large amount of hydroxyl groups that can be directly combined via intermolecular forces or hydrogen bonds. Thus, there is no need to add additional cross-linking agents that can cause pollution. The entire process does not produce toxic waste, and the experimental environment is mild. At the same time, the prepared composite aerogel has no toxic chemical reaction in the entire process of adsorbing Cr3+ pollutants in water, nor does it produce toxic substances. Through a series of experiments and analysis of the composite aerogel, we found that the aerogel can effectively adsorb Cr3+ contaminants in water and that CQDs are annihilated in this atmosphere, achieving the visualization function. In summary, the CQDs/aerogel system can be used effectively to adsorb and detect Cr3+ contaminants in water.

Poplar wood powders of 50–70 meshes were used as the cellulose source. A dialysis bag with a cut-off molecular weight of 1000 Da, a 0.2 μm organic filter, ethylenediamine (EDA), sodium chlorite, glacial acetic acid, potassium hydroxide, chromium chloride, and other laboratory grade chemicals were used as received without further purification.

Poplar wood powders were subjected to chemical pretreatment (75 °C for 1 h) using acidified sodium chlorite (pH4~5, adjusted by glacial acetic acid). This was repeated five times to remove lignin. Then, 5 wt% potassium hydroxide (90 °C for 2 h) was used to remove hemicelluloses. The above steps were repeated once to ensure complete reaction. Subsequently, the obtained 1 wt% chemically purified cellulose was nanofibrillated using a cell wall breaking machine with an output power of 1200 W for 30 min; the NFC suspension was thus generated.

The chemically purified cellulose that was prepared in the previous step served as a carbon source, and ethylenediamine was used as a modifier to prepared CQDs via the hydrothermal method. Briefly, 1 g of cellulose was thoroughly mixed with 5 mL of EDA using a magnetic stirrer. The mixture was then decanted into a polytetrafluoroethylene (PTFE) reaction kettle and heated at 180 °C for 8 h in an oven. The mixture was cooled naturally to room temperature, and the supernatant was filtered using a 0.2 μm organic filter and dialyzed using a 1000 Da dialysis bag to obtain CQDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: One-step synthesis of CQDs/NFC composite aerogel
ce:section.ce:para: The aerogel was prepared in a freeze-dryer under a vacuum atmosphere. Specifically, 5 mL of carbon dots solution was carefully transferred into a beaker that contained the NFC suspension. The mixture was magnetically stirred for 3 h to ensure uniform mixing. The mixed suspension was then poured into a beaker, which was placed in a refrigerator at −15 °C for more than 24 h to ensure complete freezing. The frozen samples were then freeze-dried using a freeze-dryer, and this resulted in the formation of carbon quantum dots/composite aerogels.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Adsorption of heavy metal ions (Cr3+) in water by CQDs/NFC composite aerogel
ce:section.ce:para:
In real life, the pH of wastewater is not constant. However, pH is an important indicator that affects adsorption. Therefore, it is necessary to add appropriate amounts of HCl and NaOH to configure metal ion solutions that have different pH values, while ensuring that the concentration of ions remains unchanged. With the use of a constant temperature air bath, the same amount of composite aerogel was placed in solution samples that had different pH values, and the effect of pH sampling on adsorption performance was measured. The best adsorption pH for independent detection was then selected. The solution and gel were sampled separately at different times, and the ion concentration and gel fluorescence intensity were tested. The mass of the adsorbent in all of the above steps was kept at a ratio of 1:2 with respect to the volume of the solution. (For example, 1 mg of aerogel was added to 2 mL of solution.) The ion concentration in the solution was measured using an inductively coupled plasma emission spectrometer (ICP-OES), and the amount of adsorption was calculated according to the following formula:
(1)
q
e
=
(
C 0 −
C
e
)
• V
m
where C0 (mg·L−1) and Ce (mg·L−1) respectively represent the initial concentration and the adsorption concentration, V (L) represents the volume of the adsorption solution, and m (g) represents the weight of the aerogel.

At the same time, to further study the adsorption behavior of the aerogel, pseudo-first-order and pseudo-second-order dynamic equations were used to simulate the kinetics of absorption by the aerogel. The pseudo-first-order dynamic equation is expressed as follows (Lagergren, 1898):
(2)
q
t
=
q
e
×
(
1 −
e
- k
1
× t
)

The pseudo-second-order dynamic equation is expressed as (Ho & McKay, 1999):
(3)
q
t
=
q
e
2
×
k
2
× t
1 +
q
e
×
k
2
× t
where qe and qt are respectively the adsorption equilibrium and adsorption capacity at time t (mg·g−1). t is the adsorption time (min). k1 is the pseudo-first-order adsorption rate constant (min−1), and k2 is the pseudo-second-order adsorption rate constant (min−1). Both formulas (2) and (3) can be linearly converted into the following expressions:
ln

)

The flow chart was drawn using ChemDraw (Cambridge Soft, USA). Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images were obtained using a JEM 2100 transmission electron microscope (JEOL Ltd., Tokyo, Japan) under an acceleration voltage of 200 kV. Fluorescence microscopy images were obtained using an upright high-resolution fluorescence microscope (Axio Imager A2, Germany). The excitation wavelengths were 405, 488, and 555 nm, and the light source for the fluorescence microscopy experiments was a mercury lamp with a fluorescent filter. Surface area and porosity of the nanomaterials were tested using an ASAP 2020 V4.01 (V4.01H) accelerated surface area and porosimetry system (Micromeritics Instrument Corp.). The specific surface area was measured using the Brunauer-Emmett-Teller (BET) method, and the pore distribution was analyzed using the Barrett-Joyner-Halenda (BJH) method. The photoluminescence (PL) spectrum of the material was recorded using an F-7000 fluorescence spectrophotometer equipped with a 150W xenon lamp as the excitation source. Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy(FT-IR) was obtained using a Nicolette 6700 instrument (Thermo Nicolet Corporation, USA). The X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was performed using a THERMO (Thermo Electron Corporation, USA). The binding energy scale was referenced by setting the C 1s transition at 284.6 eV. The X-ray diffraction (XRD) pattern was scanned using a D max/RB 2500 V X-ray diffractometer (Rigaku Co., Japan) with Cu-Ka radiation (λ = 1.54056 Å). The concentration of metal ion solution was tested using an inductively coupled plasma emission spectrometer (ICP-OES) (VDV/5110, Agilent Technologies Inc., USA).

Carbon quantum dots/composite aerogels were prepared using a green procedure, as shown in Fig. 1 A. CQDs were prepared via a hydrothermal method and were added before the formation of the aerogel. CQDs were sufficiently combined via hydrogen bonding, and then the composite aerogel is prepared in one step via freeze-drying. It is worth noting that no toxic substances were produced throughout the process, the utilization rate of the raw material is high, and there are not too many waste products. After lyophilization, the blank aerogel is a white fluffy sponge and the composite aerogel is tan. The mixture emits bright blue-green fluorescence at an excitation wavelength of 365 nm, and the blank control aerogel does not have any fluorescence emission (Fig. 1A). The fluorescence microscopy images were recorded with different excitation wavelengths, and these further confirm that the CQDs combined with the NFC aerogels (Fig. 1B). In detail, at different excitation wavelengths, the emission wavelength of the fluorescence of the excited composite is also different. The emission wavelength of the carbon dots in the aerogel is different, and this reflects the excitation-dependent emission wavelengths of the carbon dots [64,65]. Moreover, the fluorescence intensity is not the same, and this is also caused by the different wavelengths of the excitation light. This observation indicates that CQDs were successfully combined with nanocellulose aerogels.

Figs. 2 and 3
show the characteristics and spectral analysis of the CQDs. Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) provides a visual image of the carbon dot morphology doped in the gel (Fig. 2A). CQDs are distributed in solution; however, there is a certain amount of variability in the particle size distribution, and this indicates that the CQDs are partially agglomerated. Analysis of the particle sizes shows that the sizes are mainly concentrated around 6–8 nm (Fig. 2B). The diffraction lattice can be clearly observed in the high-resolution TEM (HR-TEM) image, and this indicates that the core lattice of the CQDs is a graphite phase structure (Fig. 2A). This is consistent with the characteristics of CQDs that have been reported in other literature [39].

The FT-IR spectrum of the CQDs is shown in Fig. 3. The characteristic absorption peaks of the aromatic CN heterocycle are in the range of 1250–1550 cm−1, and this represents the C–N stretching vibration. The CO stretching vibration is at 1650 cm−1, and the C–O tensile vibration is at 1050 cm−1. The peaks at 2860 cm−1 and 2930 cm−1 correspond to the stretching vibration of C–H in the alkane and that of N–H. The peak at 3240 cm−1 corresponds to the O–H stretching vibration, and these O–H groups participate in hydrogen bonding of alcohols and phenols [66].

Fig. 4 shows the fluorescence performance of the CQDs. The PL spectra reflect the wavelength dependence of the carbon dots. Specifically, when the excitation wavelength was increased from 300 nm to 400 nm, the emission wavelength changed from 400 nm to 460 nm. The peak intensity also changes, and the best excitation wavelength is 365 nm (Fig. 4A). This is consistent with the results reported in our previous work [67]. On the other hand, two peaks (around 270 nm and 330 nm) are observed in the UV–vis spectrum. The peak at 270 nm is a π-π* transition that results from aromatic sp2 hybridization, and the peak at 330 nm is an n-π* transition that is caused by a C–O bond. It is worth noting that these observation are not the same as those for CQDs that were prepared using biomass materials alone, and this should be due to the doping of amino groups [68] Optical photographs are shown in the inset of Fig. 4A. The photograph on the right was taken in a dark box under illumination of a 365 nm UV lamp. As seen, CQDs emit bright blue-green light in solution. In addition, we performed a solid fluorescence test on the composite material and compared its fluorescence intensity as a control to the intensity measured with different Cr3+ concentrations (Fig. 4B). As seen in the figure, the effect of carbon fluorescent quantum dots is retained within the material after formation of the composite material. Also, the fluorescence quenching phenomenon occurs at higher ion concentrations; with an increase in ion concentration, the fluorescence effect decays.

The scanning electron microscopy (SEM) images showed that the prepared composite aerogel was sponge-like and rich in pores. Also, the inner wall of the gel was relatively rough (Fig. 5 A), and this provided a good template for forming the composite with the CQDs such that the CQDs were uniformly dispersed inside the gel without aggregation. Therefore, it is still able to emit fluorescence in the solid state, and the fluorescence photograph shown in Fig. 1 clearly and intuitively shows the visualization of the reaction product. The N2 adsorption of the composite aerogel sample can be used to evaluate the permanent porosity and is an important indicator of the adsorption performance of a material. The composite aerogel exhibits a reversible type IV isotherm (Fig. 5B), and this is one of the main characteristics of mesoporous materials [69]. The surface area of the composite aerogel was calculated to be 479.62m2g-1, according to the BET model. The mesopore peak is around 4.8 nm, and the mesoporous pore size ranges up to 36 nm. At the same time, the pore volume was 0.457 cc/g, and the average pore diameter was 4.26 nm. The larger specific surface area indicates that nanocellulose aerogel has the potential to be used as an adsorbent material.

X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) indicates that CQDs and nanofibrillated cellulose aerogels were successfully combined (Fig. 6 A), and this is consistent with the fluorescence spectra (Fig. 3). Specifically, both samples have C1s and O1s peaks that are around 286 eV and 528 eV, respectively, and this indicates that there are nanocellulose aerogel skeleton structures composed of C and O in both samples. However, the difference is that the N1s peak appears in the composite material (Fig. 6A, i), and this peak value is around 396eV, which indicates that the CQDs that contain N successfully formed composites with the aerogels. At the same time, because CQDs also contain a large amount of C and O, increases in the C1s and O1s peak values in the composite material also indicate the successful introduction of CQDs. To further understand the chemical bonding in the composite gel, high resolution C1s and N1s XPS spectra were analyzed (Fig. 6). For the C1s spectrum, there are four independent peaks that can be separated; these are located at 284.6eV, 285.7eV, 286.6eV, and 288eV and correspond to C–C, C–N, C–O, and CN/CO bonds, respectively, in the material. For the N1s spectrum, peaks at 399.1 eV and 401.1 eV indicate the presence of C–N–C and N–H bonding, respectively, and the peak around 406.2eV corresponds to the presence of the –NO3 group [70].

Diffraction peaks of 16.5°, 21°, and 22.6° were observed in XRD spectra of both the CQDs/NFC aerogels and CQDs. For the composite aerogel, the two diffraction peaks of 16.5° and 22.6° correspond to the (110) and (200) crystal faces, respectively, and thees belong to the cellulose I type structure. This indicates that the crystal structure of lignocellulose itself did not change during the preparation. In addition, for CQDs, a diffraction peak at 21° represents a lattice structure that is similar to that of graphene, and this is consistent with the TEM image (Fig. 2).

In summary, our work shows that CQDs are successfully combined with aerogels without destroying either the aerogel porous structure or the carbon dots’ fluorescence performance. Thus, this composite material has great application prospects in the fields of liquid detection and adsorption.

We analyzed our experimental results to investigate the use of the CQDs/NFC composite aerogels for the detection and adsorption of heavy metal ions in water. Fig. 7 A succinctly illustrates the detection and adsorption process of the composite materials, and more clearly reflects its visual performance. This effect can also be observed in optical pictures (Fig. 7B).

In the adsorption process, we found an interesting phenomenon. There is a functional relationship between the fluorescence quenching performance of CQDs and the adsorption of aerogels. Fig. 9 A shows the adsorption and fluorescence properties of the composite aerogel at different concentrations. With an increase in the ion concentration in the solution, the adsorption amount of the composite aerogel gradually increases, and the fluorescence intensity gradually decreases. At this point, the amount of adsorption (qe) and the fluorescence intensity (F) can be fitted well using a polynomial:
F = − 0.0193
q
e
2
− 9.7167
q
e
+ 1673.7
, (R2 = 0.999). The fluorescence loss amount and aerogel adsorption amount are fitted as positive correlation images that are shown in Fig. 9B. This result indicates that the fluorescence effect of CQDs is related to the amount of NFC aerogel adsorption. This result indicates that it is feasible to predict the adsorption of heavy metal ions in water by measuring the fluorescence effect of CQDs.

In summary, a new CQDs composite aerogel was successfully prepared using the cellulose in natural wood as a single green source. The composite was first applied for adsorption and detection. The entire experimental process is green and does not produce toxic by-products. Also, the synthesis process successfully preserves the porous ordered structure of the aerogel as well as preserves the photoluminescence performance of CQDs. In addition, the synthesized products have good adsorption of Cr3+ ions at different pH values. More importantly, the data obtained in this experiment show for the first time that the fluorescence intensity of CQDs can be used for detecting heavy metal ion adsorption behavior of NFC aerogel and that there is a negative correlation function behavior of linear fitting. This work presents the preparation of a new type of green adsorbent and also provides a new idea for further exploring the synergy between adsorption and fluorescence.
Zihui Song and Xueqi Chen contributed equally to this work.

This work was financially supported by the National Key R&D Program of China (NO. 2018YFD0600302), applying in the raw material purchase, sample preparation, analysis and collection of data and so on.
Key Lab of Bio-based Material Science and Technology (Ministry of Education), College of Material Science and Engineering, Northeast Forestry University, Harbin 150040, PR China.

We confirm that this author is the sole contact for the Editorial process (including EVISE and direct communications with the office). She is responsible for communicating with the other authors, about progress, submissions of revisions and final approval of proofs.

Zihui Song: Formal analysis, Writing - original draft. Xueqi Chen: Writing - review & editing. Xinchao Gong: Software. Xing Gao: Methodology. Qian Dai: Validation. Tat Thang Nguyen: Visualization. Minghui Guo: Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by the National Key R&D Program of China (NO. 2018YFD0600302).
